title: practical english usage - Part 191
content:
If prices will really come down in a few months, I'm not going to buy one now.

If Anna won't be here on Thursday, we'd better cancel the meeting.

now that..

We   use will with if when we  are saying 'if it is true now that...' or 'if we  know 2 'If it  is  true    now    that   .. / We'll go home now if it will make you feel better, (result) - We'll go home now if you get the car. (condition) it follows the gift of money.) I'll give you £100  if it'll help you to get home. (The return home is a result - it must happen first.) - I'll give you £100  if I win the lottery. (Winning the lottery is a condition - actions - to mean 'if this will be the later result’ . Compare: We   use will with if to talk about what will happen because of possible future 1  results But in certain situations we  use if... will.

I'll phone you if I have time, (     .. .if J will have time.) to the future (►  231).

We   normally use a present tense with if (and most other conjunctions) to refer If you will eat so much  .. .

If you will come this way...

I don't know if I'll be ready in time.

If Anna won't be here, we'd better cancel the meeting.

I'll give you £100  if it will help you to get home.

If only she hadn't told the police, everything would have been all right.

c  past perfect to talk about the past If only somebody would smile!

If only it would stop raining we could go out.

b  would + infinitive (without to) to talk about the future If grammar •  244  other points You touch me again, I'll kick your teeth in.

You want to get in, you pay like everybody else. (= If you want...) especially when the speaker is making conditions or threats.

If is sometimes left out at the beginning of a sentence in a conversational style, 4 leaving      out   if:  conversational           - You     want      to   get    in,   you     pay.

But for your help, I don't know what I'd have done.

But for can be used to mean ‘ if it were not for’  or 'if it had not been for'.

If it hadn't been for your help, I don't know what I'd have done.

To  talk about the past we  use If it had not been for.

If it wasn't/weren't for the children, we could go skiing next week.

(= Without his wife's money,...) If it wasn't/weren't for his wife's money he'd never be a director.

This structure is used to say that one event or situation changes everything.

3 if  it  was/were         not    for For the difference between was and were after if, ►    239.4.

If 1 knew her name... (     If I were to-know her name-...) continuing situations.

This structure is not normally used with verbs like be or know, which  refer to If you were to move your chair a bit, we could all sit down.

It can be used to make a suggestion sound less direct, and so more polite.

What would we do if I was/were to lose my job?

(= If the boss came ...) If the boss was/were to come in now, we'd be in real trouble.

This is another way of talking about unreal or imaginary future events.

2 if  ...  was/were         to If I should run into Daniel...

now unusual.

Should can be used after (/in British English with a similar meaning, but this is If you happen to pass a supermarket, perhaps you could pick up some eggs.

If I happen to run into Daniel, I'll tell him  to call you.

.. . happen to . .. after if.

We   can suggest that something is unlikely, or not particularly probable, by using 1  if.  .. happen        to 244other     points eating so much  ..

If you WILL eat so much,  it's not surprising you feel ill. (= ‘ If you insist on Stressed will can be used after if to criticise people's habits or choices.

5  insistence:      If  you     WILL  eat    so   much      ...

grammar •  244  other points If I'm a bit sleepy, it's because I was up all night.

An if clause can be used to admit a fact when giving a reason for it.

11   admitting       facts    with    if I wouldn't marry you if you were the last man in the world.

I'll finish this job if it takes all night.

We   can use if to mean 'even if (►  452.4).

10 if  meaning       'even    if' If she can't come to us, then we'll have to go and see her.
